A.A. OFFICIALS.
It is a frequent complain in the mouths
of Irish Ireland advertisers that they do
not receive nearly as much support from
the general body of Gaels as they are
itled to. Now we want the members
and officials of the G.A.A., by their
actions, to disprove this contention, and
disabuse the minds of traders of this idea.
‘The G.A.A. is definitely pledged to sup-
port the Irish industrial revival. Tn our

of advertisers who are rendering practical

the officers and members of the G.A.A.

GAA PATRONS

‘To the Editor of the ‘Gaelic Athlete.””

Dear Sir,—It is pleasing to sec that a
motion is coming before the Convention
to have the original letters of the patrons
of the Association published in the Rule
Book with that of Archbishop Croke.
These letters all speak of the passionate
desire of those patrons for an Independent
Ireland in thought, word and deed, and
are in striking contrast to some of the
patrons whose names are at present on
the list. One of these, Mr. William
O'Brien, M-E.P., did extraordinary ser-
vice to Ireland, and his early days are an
inspiration to the men of to-day to tread
the path of thorns; but certainly his later
conduct makes it 4 scandal for the Asso-
Ciation to allow his name appear on the

list of patrons. He was the first to
become an active agent for those who
hunted the chairman of the Cork County

Board out of Ireland, and hounded Sean
O'Hegarty to the mountains and then to
jail, because he-was a Gael and not a
west-Britain, William O'Brien's. paper
is putrid; and on one occasion it had a
vicious felon-setting leader pointing out
that the Association was playing hurley
instead of going out to avenge Belgium.
That’ same paper never condemned or
invited Trishmen to avenge Bachelor's
Walk. I feel ashamed that the Associ
tion should allow his name on the list of
patrons, while casting out his dupes as
being unworthy of membership, and I
think that the Congress on Easter Sun-
day should remove the stigma. O'Brien
‘was an inspiration ‘‘when we were boys,"”
and used to boast of his gua-running
exploits. Now, when he is tamed: by
prosperity, he cails on the youth of Ireland
to forget’ Thomas Davis and follow
General Parsons. The G.A.A. is. no
place for such a one whose followers are
more flunky than the seonins, and were
supporters of the removal’ of Kuno
Meyer's name from the roll of Freeman
of Cork City, Comune m Dusan,

CLARE.

In connection with the All-Ireland
Junior Final, the following appeal is
issued: “*On the asth April the Clare
Junior Hurling team will battle for the
‘Championship of Ireland against Queen's
County, at Croke Park, Dublin, Of our
team’s achievement in the 1914 Champion-
ship we all felt justly proud. Hand in
hand they went’ and won the Munster
Championship, defeating the crack teams
of the province, thus proving to Ireland
that, with training, the Clare hurlers were
fit to hold their own against any combina-
tion. Our senior hurlers are now Chiam-
pions of Ireland, and the splendid -cup
presented by the Railway Company ia’
connection with it fs in the custody of Mr.
Amby. Power (Quin). ‘The Junior team
has just another ditch to leap ere the
pinnacle of fame is reached —just one
more leap successfully and Clare, for once
at least, is out of the mire, and her flag
is pinned to the foremost mast—the glori-
‘ous flag of victory will float over the old
county. To see this happy day dawn in
Clare is the ambition of us all. Yet, as
we have pointed out, before it dawns,
another’battle must be waged and_won,
and to make ready for the ght the sinews
of war areinecessary.
appeal to the Gaels of Clare to come to
our aid. It may be'a last appeal there-
fore, let us be generous and give freely.
